description: Days, months, seasons, and time expressions

items.notes: From 'maan' (earth/land) + 'antai'. Days of the week are not capitalized in Finnish.
examples: Maanantai on viikon ensimmäinen päivä. — Monday is the first day of the week.

items.notes: Named after the Norse god Týr. Not capitalized in Finnish.
examples: Nähdään tiistaina! — See you on Tuesday!

items.notes: Literally 'mid-week' (keski = middle, viikko = week). Not capitalized in Finnish.
examples: Keskiviikko on viikon keskellä. — Wednesday is in the middle of the week.

items.notes: Named after the Norse god Thor. Not capitalized in Finnish.
examples: Torstaina menemme kauppaan. — On Thursday we go to the store.

items.notes: From the Norse goddess Frigg. Not capitalized in Finnish.
examples: Perjantai on viikon paras päivä! — Friday is the best day of the week!

items.notes: Related to the word 'lauantai' from old Swedish. Not capitalized in Finnish.
examples: Lauantaina on vapaapäivä. — Saturday is a day off.

items.notes: From 'sunnun' (sun) + 'tai'. Not capitalized in Finnish.
examples: Sunnuntaina lepäämme. — On Sunday we rest.

items.notes: Literally 'oak month' (tammi = oak, kuu = month). Finnish months are not capitalized.
examples: Tammikuu on vuoden ensimmäinen kuukausi. — January is the first month of the year.

items.notes: Literally 'pearl month' (helmi = pearl, kuu = month). Refers to frozen droplets resembling pearls.
examples: Helmikuussa on ystävänpäivä. — In February is Valentine's Day.

items.notes: Literally 'earth month' (maalis- relates to earth/ground). The ground starts to show through the snow.
examples: Maaliskuussa lumi alkaa sulaa. — In March the snow starts to melt.

items.notes: From 'huhta' (slash-and-burn clearing). Relates to spring preparation of fields.
examples: Huhtikuussa tulee kevät. — In April spring comes.

items.notes: From 'touko' (spring sowing). The month of planting crops.
examples: Toukokuussa kukat alkavat kukkia. — In May flowers start to bloom.

items.notes: Literally 'summer month' (kesä = summer, kuu = month). Midsummer (juhannus) is celebrated in June.
examples: Kesäkuussa on juhannuspäivä. — In June is Midsummer Day.

items.notes: Literally 'hay month' (heinä = hay, kuu = month). Traditional time for hay harvesting.
examples: Heinäkuu on usein vuoden lämpimin kuukausi. — July is often the warmest month of the year.

items.notes: Literally 'harvest month' (elo = harvest/grain, kuu = month). The main grain harvest time.
examples: Elokuussa koulu alkaa. — In August school starts.

items.notes: Literally 'autumn month' (syys- from syksy = autumn, kuu = month). Autumn begins.
examples: Syyskuussa lehdet muuttuvat keltaisiksi. — In September the leaves turn yellow.

items.notes: Literally 'mud month' (loka = mud/dirt, kuu = month). The rainy, muddy time of autumn.
examples: Lokakuussa sataa paljon. — In October it rains a lot.

items.notes: Literally 'death month' (marras = dying/dead, kuu = month). Nature dies before winter.
examples: Marraskuu on pimeä kuukausi. — November is a dark month.

items.notes: Literally 'Christmas month' (joulu = Christmas, kuu = month).
examples: Joulukuussa juhlimme joulua. — In December we celebrate Christmas.

items.notes: Stem: kevää- (e.g., keväällä = in spring). An important season in Finland as days get longer.
examples: Keväällä linnut palaavat. — In spring the birds return.

items.notes: Finnish summers feature midnight sun in the north. Kesällä = in summer.
examples: Kesällä on lämmin. — In summer it is warm.

items.notes: Also called 'ruska' when referring to the colorful autumn foliage. Syksyllä = in autumn.
examples: Syksyllä lehdet putoavat. — In autumn the leaves fall.

items.notes: Stem: talve- (e.g., talvella = in winter). Finnish winters are long and snowy.
examples: Talvella sataa lunta. — In winter it snows.

items.notes: A very common word. Also means 'quite' as an adverb (e.g., aika hyvä = quite good).
examples: Minulla ei ole aikaa. — I don't have time.

items.notes: Also means 'lesson' in school context (e.g., matematiikan tunti = math lesson).
examples: Odota yksi tunti. — Wait one hour.

items.notes: Partitive form: minuuttia (e.g., viisi minuuttia = five minutes).
examples: Bussi tulee viiden minuutin päästä. — The bus comes in five minutes.

items.notes: Partitive form: sekuntia. Similar to English loanword.
examples: Odota sekunti! — Wait a second!

items.notes: Very common. 'Hyvää päivää' = good day (formal greeting). Päivällä = during the day.
examples: Tänään on kaunis päivä. — Today is a beautiful day.

items.notes: Partitive: viikkoa. Finnish weeks start on Monday. Viikolla = during the week.
examples: Nähdään ensi viikolla! — See you next week!

items.notes: The general word for month. Literally 'moon period' (kuu = moon, kausi = period). Specific months end in -kuu.
examples: Yksi kuukausi on noin neljä viikkoa. — One month is about four weeks.

items.notes: Stem: vuode- (genitive: vuoden). Partitive: vuotta (e.g., kaksi vuotta = two years).
examples: Hyvää uutta vuotta! — Happy New Year!

items.notes: From 'tänä' (this) + 'päivänä' (day) contracted. Very frequently used.
examples: Mitä teet tänään? — What are you doing today?

items.notes: Related to 'huomen' (morning/tomorrow). 'Huominen' is the adjective form (tomorrow's).
examples: Huomenna on perjantai. — Tomorrow is Friday.

items.notes: Eilinen = yesterday's (adjective). Toissapäivänä = the day before yesterday.
examples: Eilen satoi vettä. — Yesterday it rained.

items.notes: One of the shortest and most common Finnish words. Nykyään = nowadays.
examples: Tule tänne nyt! — Come here now!

items.notes: Very common adverb. Ainoa = only/sole (related adjective).
examples: Hän on aina iloinen. — He/she is always happy.

items.notes: Used with a negative verb to mean 'never': en koskaan = I never. In questions it means 'ever': oletko koskaan? = have you ever?
examples: En ole koskaan käynyt Suomessa. — I have never been to Finland.

items.notes: Used to tell time: 'Kello on kolme' = It is three o'clock. Also means watch/clock as an object.
examples: Paljonko kello on? — What time is it?
